feed.title: Combined summary - Mapping the design space of sibling-aware covenants

entry.title: Combined summary - Mapping the design space of sibling-aware covenants
entry.summary: The discourse on the OP_CTV and OP_TEMPLATEHASH proposals highlights several techniques for implementing sibling-aware covenants, a concept utilized in advanced blockchain systems like BitVM, Ark, and LN-Symmetry. Sibling-aware covenants are instrumental for adding conditions or invalidating a covenant through commitments to sibling transactions. OP_TEMPLATEHASH (TH) is preferred over OP_CTV due to its simplicity and lack of need for pre-computation, despite OP_CTV's unique approach of committing to scriptSigs. Exploring the technicalities, there are multiple methods proposed to integrate sibling-aware covenants with TH. These include commitments to all scriptSigs, utilizing Dynamic MuHash for indexed prevouts, restricting execution to the first input, and committing only to the next available prevout if present. An additional method involves creating a new witness program called Pay-to-Prevout-Anchor (P2PA), which mandates that an output cannot be created unless it matches the hash of a corresponding prevout. Other introspective opcodes like OP_TXHASH and OP_CAT are suggested for broader application in transaction scrutiny. One notable aspect of these methods is their potential impact on blockchain protocols such as Ark. In reality, the benefit to Ark from sibling commitments appears minimal, primarily serving to reduce costs associated with spending a pre-created connector output in specific transactions. The actual savings, however, would necessitate an explicit Pay-to-Bare-Tapscript or Pay-to-TemplateHash witness program and would only materialize under certain conditions, such as when an operator needs to publish a forfeit transaction. For further exploration of contract-level relative timelocks and their implications for sibling-aware covenants, particularly in the context of LN-Symmetry, one can refer to discussions and research available at [Delving into Bitcoin](https://delvingbitcoin.org/t/contract-level-relative-timelocks-or-lets-talk-about-ancestry-proofs-and-singletons/1353). This area remains ripe for further investigation and development, especially considering the ongoing evolution of Layer 2 protocols and their requirements for more complex transaction capabilities.
